register_notice: Internal only — never linked from the public dashboard. See dfapti/rules/factualism-audit.md, 'Target Visibility Gate'. A candidate is promoted to a public case only once at least one source_evidence entry is independently verified to the same bar as an ACCEPTED evidence record.

candidates.name: Canva Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 26-090MR Canva Group pays $792,000 in infringement notices for failing to lodge financial reports on time

candidates.public_interest_reason: Named in an ASIC media release as one of four Canva Group entities, paying an infringement notice for allegedly failing to lodge an audited financial report by the statutory deadline — a corporate financial-transparency compliance matter.
risk_indicators: Received an ASIC infringement notice ($198,000) for alleged late lodgement of an audited financial report, per 26-090MR

candidates.name: Liberty Primary Metals Australia Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 25-141MR Three GFG Alliance Group companies ordered to lodge outstanding annual reports with ASIC

candidates.public_interest_reason: Named in ASIC media release 25-141MR (and the preceding 25-113MR) as a GFG Alliance Group company subject to Supreme Court of NSW orders over alleged failure to lodge annual financial reports.
risk_indicators: Subject of Supreme Court of NSW civil proceedings (commenced June 2025) and consent orders (21 August 2025) over alleged failure to lodge its FY2024 annual report, per 25-141MR/25-113MR
Reported to be in voluntary administration as of a subsequent ASIC published notice

Compliance update - January to March 2026 (Clean Energy Regulator, Safeguard Mechanism excess emissions situations)

candidates.public_interest_reason: Named in ASIC media release 25-141MR (and the preceding 25-113MR) as a GFG Alliance Group company subject to Supreme Court of NSW orders over alleged failure to lodge annual financial reports.

In an excess emissions situation under the Safeguard Mechanism as at the 1 April 2026 compliance deadline, per the Clean Energy Regulator's January-March 2026 compliance update

candidates.name: JustAnswer LLC

source_evidence.title: JustAnswer in Court for alleged misleading prices, subscription trap and government affiliation claims

candidates.public_interest_reason: Subject of Federal Court proceedings brought by the ACCC (ACCC v JustAnswer LLC, VID1259 of 2025), ordered to pay $10 million in penalties for allegedly misleading consumers about subscription pricing (a '$2' offer that led to undisclosed ongoing charges of $50-90/month) and falsely implying affiliation with the Fair Work Ombudsman.
risk_indicators: Ordered by the Federal Court to pay $10 million in penalties in ACCC v JustAnswer LLC (VID1259 of 2025)
ACCC alleged misleading pricing representations and false government-affiliation claims

candidates.name: Vacaciones eDreams, S.L

source_evidence.title: Online travel agency eDreams pays penalties and gives undertaking over allegedly misleading subscription prices

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid $59,400 in ACCC infringement notices and gave a court-enforceable s.87B undertaking over allegedly misleading subscription pricing practices (advertising a subscription as '$0.00'/'FREE' without adequately disclosing conditions, and not prominently displaying total annual cost).
risk_indicators: Paid $59,400 in ACCC infringement notices for allegedly misleading subscription pricing claims
Gave the ACCC a court-enforceable undertaking (s.87B) admitting conduct contravened or likely contravened the Australian Consumer Law

candidates.name: Christofi Investments

source_evidence.title: Ugg boot retailer pays penalties for strikethrough pricing claims

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid $39,600 in ACCC infringement notices for allegedly misleading 'strikethrough' pricing claims on ugg-boot retail websites it owns/administers (Original Ugg Australia Classic, Ugg Originals).
risk_indicators: Paid $39,600 in ACCC infringement notices for allegedly misleading strikethrough pricing claims

candidates.name: Energex

source_evidence.title: Administrative undertakings and penalties paid by Energex and TasNetworks for alleged breaches of life support obligations

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid a penalty (reported as $60,000 in one AER source; an earlier related release cites $100,000/$140,000 total across the two distributors — treat the exact split as MEDIUM pending direct document confirmation) for alleged breaches of life support customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Rules — obligations that exist specifically to protect customers dependent on electricity supply for life support equipment.
risk_indicators: Alleged breach of life support customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Rules, resulting in an AER penalty and administrative undertaking

candidates.name: TasNetworks

source_evidence.title: Administrative undertakings and penalties paid by Energex and TasNetworks for alleged breaches of life support obligations

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid a penalty (reported as $80,000 in one AER source; figures vary slightly across related AER releases — treat as MEDIUM pending direct document confirmation) for alleged breaches of life support customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Rules.
risk_indicators: Alleged breach of life support customer obligations under the National Energy Retail Rules, resulting in an AER penalty and administrative undertaking

candidates.name: AGL Retail Energy Limited

source_evidence.title: AGL penalised $25 million for breaches of overcharging rules related to Centrepay payments

candidates.public_interest_reason: One of four AGL Energy Limited subsidiaries the Federal Court found breached overcharging rules relating to Centrepay payments (a government bill-paying service for Centrelink recipients), resulting in the largest total penalty ($25 million) ever imposed for breaches of the National Energy Laws. 483 Centrepay customers were overcharged and 16,156 breaches found for failing to inform/refund within required timeframes. The other three subsidiary entities named in the same action were not independently identified by name in the sources reviewed this pass, so are not separately recorded as candidates.
risk_indicators: Found by the Federal Court to have breached National Energy Retail Rules overcharging obligations relating to Centrepay (welfare-payment) customers
Subject to the largest total penalty ($25 million) ever imposed for National Energy Laws breaches
Decision under appeal to the Full Federal Court as of the source reviewed (heard November 2025, pending)

candidates.name: Alinta Energy Retail Sales Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Alinta fined $1.089 million for alleged mishandling of Centrepay payments

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid $1,089,000 in AER penalties (a record 15 infringement notices) for allegedly overcharging Centrepay customers (a government bill-paying service for Centrelink recipients) across NSW, Qld and SA between 2019-2024, and failing to inform/refund them within required timeframes.
risk_indicators: Paid $1,089,000 in AER infringement notice penalties for alleged Centrepay overcharging and failure to timely inform/refund welfare-payment customers

candidates.name: CAM Engineering and Construction Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: CAM Engineering and Construction Pty Ltd ordered to pay penalty for failing to join Energy and Water Ombudsman NSW scheme

candidates.public_interest_reason: Ordered by the Federal Court to pay a $250,000 penalty for allegedly failing to become a member of the Energy and Water Ombudsman NSW scheme, in breach of the National Energy Retail Law — a consumer-access-to-redress compliance matter.
risk_indicators: Ordered by the Federal Court to pay a $250,000 penalty for alleged failure to join a mandatory ombudsman scheme under the National Energy Retail Law

candidates.name: OnePath Custodians Pty Limited

source_evidence.title: APRA issues $10.7 million infringement notices and accepts court enforceable undertaking from OPC

candidates.public_interest_reason: Paid $10,704,600 under APRA infringement notices for alleged breaches of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 (failing to invest members' default superannuation contributions in MySuper products), and gave APRA a court enforceable undertaking to rectify compliance deficiencies and compensate members. OPC is one of four superannuation trustees owned by Insignia Financial Ltd (formerly IOOF Holdings Limited), with approximately 700,000 members and over $37 billion in funds under management — a matter with direct financial consequences for a large number of superannuation members.
risk_indicators: Paid $10,704,600 under APRA infringement notices for alleged SIS Act breaches (failure to direct default contributions to MySuper products)
Gave APRA a court enforceable undertaking to rectify compliance deficiencies and compensate affected members

candidates.name: Union Standard International Group Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 26-117MR Federal Court orders record $300 million penalties in ASIC's case over 'egregious' Union Standard and CFD operator misconduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: Collapsed CFD issuer found by the Federal Court to have engaged in systemic unconscionable conduct (with its former authorised representatives) between 2018-2020; ASIC's media release describes penalties totalling $300.2 million as the highest ever secured in an ASIC matter, with customer losses exceeding $83 million.
risk_indicators: Subject of Federal Court-ordered penalties totalling $300.2 million (ASIC's largest-ever recorded penalty in a single matter) for systemic unconscionable conduct 2018-2020

candidates.name: Maxi EFX Global AU Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 26-117MR Federal Court orders record $300 million penalties in ASIC's case over 'egregious' Union Standard and CFD operator misconduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: Traded as EuropeFX; a former authorised representative of Union Standard International Group found by the Federal Court to have engaged in systemic unconscionable conduct, part of the $300.2 million combined penalty action, with customers of EuropeFX and TradeFred together losing over $83 million.
risk_indicators: Found by the Federal Court to have engaged in systemic unconscionable conduct as an authorised representative of Union Standard, part of a $300.2 million combined penalty action

candidates.name: BrightAU Capital Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 26-117MR Federal Court orders record $300 million penalties in ASIC's case over 'egregious' Union Standard and CFD operator misconduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: Traded as TradeFred; a former authorised representative of Union Standard International Group found by the Federal Court to have engaged in systemic unconscionable conduct, part of the $300.2 million combined penalty action.
risk_indicators: Found by the Federal Court to have engaged in systemic unconscionable conduct as an authorised representative of Union Standard, part of a $300.2 million combined penalty action

candidates.name: Qteq Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Oil and gas services company Qteq and its chairman collectively fined $6 million for attempted cartel conduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: Queensland-based oil and gas services company fined $5 million by the Federal Court (in proceedings brought by the ACCC) for attempted cartel conduct between 2017-2019, attempting to induce suppliers into arrangements including provisions not to supply services to large oil and gas companies and to rig a multi-million-dollar tender.
risk_indicators: Fined $5 million by the Federal Court for attempted cartel conduct (ACCC proceedings)
Its executive chairman was separately fined $1 million, described by the ACCC as the highest individual competition-law penalty in Australia

candidates.name: OneSteel Manufacturing Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: Compliance update - January to March 2026 (Clean Energy Regulator, Safeguard Mechanism excess emissions situations)

candidates.public_interest_reason: Operator of the Whyalla Steelworks facility (currently in external administration, part of the former GFG Alliance group), named by the Clean Energy Regulator as one of three facilities in an excess emissions situation under the Safeguard Mechanism as at the 1 April 2026 compliance deadline. Completed a partial surrender of 198,003 units to reduce its excess position but remained non-compliant at the deadline.
risk_indicators: In an excess emissions situation under the Safeguard Mechanism as at the 1 April 2026 compliance deadline, per the Clean Energy Regulator's January-March 2026 compliance update
Completed only a partial unit surrender (198,003 units) toward its excess emissions position

candidates.name: Sunshine Loans Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: 24-074MR Court finds Sunshine Loans charged customers prohibited fees
SunshineLoans Pty Ltd v Australian Securities and Investments Commission [2026] HCA 8

candidates.public_interest_reason: Federal Court found Sunshine Loans Pty Ltd contravened the National Credit Code and National Consumer Credit Protection Act 2009 by charging a prohibited $35 'amendment fee' on over 670,000 small amount credit contracts between 1 July 2016 and 2 November 2020, in proceedings brought by ASIC seeking $10.5 million in penalties. The company's application to have the trial judge recuse himself from the penalty hearing (on apprehended-bias grounds) was dismissed by the High Court of Australia on 18 March 2026 ([2026] HCA 8).
risk_indicators: Found by the Federal Court to have contravened the National Credit Code/NCCP Act by charging a prohibited fee on over 670,000 small amount credit contracts
Subject of ASIC penalty proceedings seeking $10.5 million
Unsuccessfully sought recusal of the trial judge from the penalty hearing; application dismissed by the High Court of Australia, [2026] HCA 8

candidates.name: Mercer Superannuation (Australia) Limited

source_evidence.title: 24-173MR ASIC's first greenwashing case results in landmark $11.3 million penalty for Mercer

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Mercer Superannuation (Australia) Limited to pay an $11.3 million penalty — ASIC's first successful greenwashing civil penalty case — after Mercer admitted to making misleading statements that seven 'Sustainable Plus' superannuation investment options excluded investments in carbon-intensive industries such as thermal coal, when adequate systems were not in place to ensure the ESG claims were accurate or that exclusions were enforced.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $11.3 million for greenwashing conduct, admitted by the company
Court also made an adverse publicity order requiring disclosure of the contraventions
Root cause found to be inadequate systems to verify and enforce ESG exclusion claims

candidates.name: Vanguard Investments Australia Ltd

source_evidence.title: 24-213MR ASIC's Vanguard greenwashing action results in record $12.9 million penalty

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered Vanguard Investments Australia to pay a $12.9 million penalty — the largest greenwashing penalty imposed in Australia to date — after Vanguard admitted that approximately 74% of the securities (by market value) in its Ethically Conscious Global Aggregate Bond Index Fund had not actually been researched or screened against the ESG exclusion criteria it had told investors would be applied.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $12.9 million, the largest greenwashing penalty in Australia to date
Admitted approximately 74% of the fund's securities by market value were not researched or screened against its stated ESG criteria
This was ASIC's second successful greenwashing civil penalty action

candidates.name: LGSS Pty Ltd (trustee for Active Super)

source_evidence.title: 25-042MR Active Super ordered to pay $10.5 million penalty in ASIC's third greenwashing court action

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered LGSS Pty Ltd, as trustee for the Active Super fund, to pay a $10.5 million penalty — ASIC's third successful greenwashing civil penalty action — after finding Active Super had claimed in its marketing to eliminate investments posing environmental or community risk (including gambling, coal mining and oil tar sands) while continuing to hold direct investments in companies such as Shell Plc and Whitehaven Coal.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $10.5 million for false or misleading ESG representations
Found to have retained direct investments (e.g. Shell Plc, Whitehaven Coal) in categories it had publicly claimed to exclude
ASIC's third successful greenwashing civil penalty action

candidates.name: Fiducian Investment Management Services Limited

source_evidence.title: 25-225MR ASIC acts against ESG investment fund responsible entity alleging governance failures and misleading conduct

candidates.public_interest_reason: ASIC commenced its fourth greenwashing civil penalty action against Fiducian Investment Management Services Limited (FIMSL) as responsible entity of the Diversified Social Aspirations Fund, alleging FIMSL failed to act with care and diligence, made misleading statements in the fund's PDS about monitoring underlying investments, and failed to address investor concerns that the fund held investments in companies including BHP, Rio Tinto, Woodside Energy, Newcrest Mining and Orica. FIMSL has agreed to make admissions but a Statement of Agreed Facts and penalty had not been finalised or approved by the court as of the most recent reporting.
risk_indicators: Subject of ASIC's fourth greenwashing civil penalty action, alleging governance failures and misleading PDS statements
FIMSL has agreed to make admissions of contraventions of the Corporations Act and ASIC Act
Proceedings not yet concluded: no court-approved penalty as of the most recent reporting

candidates.name: Netwealth Superannuation Services Pty Ltd

source_evidence.title: APRA accepts court enforceable undertaking from Netwealth

candidates.public_interest_reason: APRA accepted a court enforceable undertaking from Netwealth Superannuation Services Pty Ltd (trustee of a superannuation platform with around 115,000 member accounts and over $40 billion in funds under management) after a thematic review found material weaknesses in its investment governance framework, including deficiencies in onboarding due diligence and monitoring of investment options and management of outsourced investment services and related conflicts of interest. In parallel, ASIC commenced separate Corporations Act proceedings against Netwealth and accepted its own court-enforceable undertaking relating to First Guardian investor compensation.
risk_indicators: Subject of an APRA court enforceable undertaking following a thematic review identifying material weaknesses in investment governance
Findings included deficiencies in due diligence, monitoring of investment options, and management of related-party conflicts of interest
Also subject of separate, parallel ASIC Corporations Act proceedings and a court-enforceable undertaking concerning First Guardian investor compensation

candidates.name: United Super Pty Ltd (trustee for Cbus)

source_evidence.title: APRA accepts court enforceable undertaking from Cbus and launches investigation

candidates.public_interest_reason: APRA accepted a court enforceable undertaking from United Super Pty Ltd, trustee for the Construction and Building Unions Superannuation Fund (Cbus, over 920,000 member accounts and around $100 billion in funds under management), after a prudential review found significant and persistent weaknesses in its operational risk management framework across four areas: operational risk management, information/IT resilience, insurance claims processing and administration, and expenditure management. APRA separately launched an investigation into United Super for possible breaches of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993, focused on expenditure management practices.
risk_indicators: Subject of an APRA court enforceable undertaking following findings of significant and persistent operational risk management weaknesses
Undertaking spans four areas: operational risk management, IT resilience, insurance claims administration, and expenditure management
Separately under APRA investigation for possible breaches of the SIS Act relating to expenditure management practices

candidates.name: Fiducian Portfolio Services Limited

source_evidence.title: APRA imposes additional licence conditions on Fiducian

candidates.public_interest_reason: APRA imposed additional licence conditions (effective 2 April 2026) on Fiducian Portfolio Services Limited, trustee of the Fiducian Superannuation Fund (approximately 9,779 member accounts, over $3.1 billion in funds under management), after a review identified concerns including insufficiently rigorous investment selection criteria and due diligence, weak monitoring/reporting frameworks, management of related-party conflicts of interest, and deficiencies in board governance and oversight. Note: this is a distinct legal entity from Fiducian Investment Management Services Limited (T-AU-2026-00034), which is the subject of a separate ASIC greenwashing action against a different Fiducian-group entity.
risk_indicators: Subject of additional APRA licence conditions effective 2 April 2026 following a review of investment governance
Findings included insufficiently rigorous investment selection/due diligence criteria and weak monitoring frameworks
Findings also included deficiencies in board governance and management of related-party conflicts of interest

candidates.name: FIIG Securities Limited

source_evidence.title: 26-021MR ASIC action sees FIIG Securities ordered to pay $2.5 million over cyber security failures

candidates.public_interest_reason: The Federal Court ordered FIIG Securities Limited to pay a $2.5 million penalty plus $500,000 towards ASIC's costs — the first civil penalty imposed on an Australian Financial Services licensee for cybersecurity-related contraventions of its general licensee obligations under section 912A of the Corporations Act 2001. FIIG admitted that over a four-year period it failed to have adequate cybersecurity measures in place, which contributed to a May 2023 cyberattack in which approximately 385GB of data, including sensitive client information, was exfiltrated from its systems.
risk_indicators: Federal Court civil penalty of $2.5 million, admitted by the company — the first such penalty for cybersecurity-related licensee obligation failures in Australia
Admitted inadequate cybersecurity measures over a four-year period prior to a May 2023 data breach affecting approximately 385GB of data including sensitive client information
Court also ordered an independent-expert compliance programme to review its cyber security and resilience systems
